threats.title: Arbitrary Delegation Overwrite in AgentVeToken
threats.hypothesis_description: The `stake` function allows any caller to arbitrarily overwrite the delegation of any other user's veTokens by passing their address as the `receiver` parameter.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `_delegate` function in OpenZeppelin's `ERC20Votes` changes the delegation for the entire balance of the specified account. Because `stake` accepts a user-supplied `receiver` and passes it directly to `_delegate(receiver, delegatee)`, an attacker can stake a dust amount (e.g., 1 wei) to a victim's address and forcibly redirect their entire voting power to an attacker-controlled validator.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; this is a core logic flaw in the contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _delegate(receiver, delegatee) called with user-supplied receiver without authorization check

preconditions: Target user has an existing veToken balance and delegation

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: `_delegate(receiver, delegatee)` modifies the delegation of `receiver` based on caller input
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that `msg.sender == receiver` or that caller has allowance to manage receiver's delegation

analysis_questions: Does `_delegate` actually move the entire voting power of the receiver?
Can this be used to instantly hijack a DAO vote?
validation_requirements: Create a test where User A stakes and delegates to Validator X
Have User B call `stake(1, User A, Validator Y)`
Verify that User A's voting power is moved to Validator Y

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker identifies a whale with massive voting power. Attacker calls `stake(1, whaleAddress, attackerValidator)`. The whale's entire voting power is instantly delegated to the attacker's validator, allowing the attacker to pass malicious DAO proposals.
prerequisites: Availability of assetToken to stake 1 wei

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete compromise of agent DAOs, leading to potential theft of agent assets or malicious metadata updates
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All veToken holders and the agent ecosystem
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Users would have to notice and re-stake to fix their delegation, but attackers can automate the overwrite

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for third-party staking and delegation preservation

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Can be executed right before a crucial DAO vote

threats.title: Complete Drain of Factory Asset Tokens via Custom Token Aliasing
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can propose an application using the factory's `assetToken` as the custom token. Upon withdrawal, the factory transfers its entire balance of `assetToken` to the attacker.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentFactoryV4`, `initFromToken` allows users to propose an agent using a custom ERC20 token. If the attacker passes the factory's `assetToken` as the `tokenAddr`, it passes the `isCompatibleToken` check. When the attacker calls `withdraw`, the contract first refunds the `applicationThreshold` of `assetToken`, and then executes a second transfer for the custom token, sending `IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this))`. Since `customToken` is `assetToken`, it drains all `assetToken` deposits from all other active applications.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; logic flaw in withdrawal accounting.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Transferring entire contract balance of customToken instead of tracked deposit amount

preconditions: Factory holds assetToken from other users' active applications
assetToken passes `isCompatibleToken` checks (standard ERC20s will pass)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: It assumes the factory only holds the custom tokens deposited for this specific application, which is false if customToken == assetToken
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check preventing `tokenAddr == assetToken` in `initFromToken`, and no accounting of exact custom token deposit amount

analysis_questions: Does the double transfer in `withdraw` execute successfully?
validation_requirements: Deploy factory with assetToken
Have User A propose standard agent (deposits assetToken)
Have Attacker call `initFromToken` with assetToken
Have Attacker call `withdraw` and verify they receive User A's deposit

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker observes multiple active applications in the factory holding large amounts of assetToken. Attacker calls `initFromToken(assetToken, ...)`. In the next block, attacker calls `withdraw(id)`. The factory sends back the attacker's deposit, plus the entire remaining balance of assetToken in the factory.
prerequisites: Capital to cover `applicationThreshold` + `initialLP` for one proposal

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of all pending application deposits in the factory
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with active unexecuted proposals

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for edge cases where customToken is the same as assetToken

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Can be executed at any time
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as a massive withdrawal

threats.title: Atomic Sandwich Attack on Tax Autoswap
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_swapTax` function uses `amountOutMin = 0` when swapping accumulated tax tokens on Uniswap. An attacker can execute an atomic sandwich attack in a single transaction to extract the tax revenue.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the Base chain does not have a public mempool (preventing traditional front-running), the autoswap is triggered deterministically during a `transfer` when the tax threshold is met. An unprivileged attacker can write a smart contract that performs three steps in one transaction: 1) Sell AgentToken to tank the pool price, 2) Transfer AgentToken to trigger `_autoSwap` (which sells the protocol's tax tokens at the manipulated terrible price), 3) Buy back AgentToken at the depressed price for a profit. This systematically drains tax revenue.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; hardcoded 0 slippage.
preconditions: Uniswap pool has sufficient liquidity to manipulate

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Accepts any amount of output tokens, allowing infinite slippage
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No TWAP or oracle price check for minimum output

analysis_questions: Can the autoswap be triggered deterministically by an attacker contract?
Is the profit from the sandwich greater than the swap fees?
validation_requirements: Create an attacker contract that executes the 3-step atomic sandwich
Measure the value extracted vs the fees paid

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker monitors the `AgentToken` contract balance. When it reaches the threshold, the attacker executes a flash-loan powered transaction that skews the pool, triggers the swap, and reverse-swaps, extracting the value of the accumulated tax tokens.
prerequisites: Capital or flash loan to manipulate the Uniswap pool

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Continuous extraction of project tax revenue
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: The project treasury/DAO receives near-zero revenue

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing MEV/slippage resistance tests for autoswap

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Requires calculating profitability after gas and pool fees
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: High capital needed for pool manipulation, but flash loans can be used
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as high-slippage swaps in the pool

threats.title: Permissionless Validator Registration Inflates Scores
threats.hypothesis_description: The `addValidator` function in `AgentNftV2` is public and lacks access control, allowing anyone to register arbitrary addresses as validators and instantly receive a base score equal to the total number of past proposals.
threats.investigation_rationale: When a validator is added, `_initValidatorScore` sets their base score to `_getMaxScore(virtualId)`, which is mapped to `totalProposals(virtualId)`. Because `addValidator` is permissionless, an attacker can wait until an agent has many proposals, then register hundreds of attacker-controlled addresses as validators. Each address instantly receives a high score without having participated in any past governance, diluting legitimate validators and manipulating ELO/rewards.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; missing access modifier.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Public function modifying sensitive registry state without authentication

preconditions: Agent has a history of proposals (totalProposals > 0)

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should be restricted to the staking contract or DAO

analysis_questions: Does the base score grant actual privileges or rewards?
Can an attacker register multiple addresses cheaply?
validation_requirements: Call `addValidator` from an unrelated address
Verify the new validator receives a base score equal to `totalProposals`

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An agent has 500 past proposals. Attacker generates 100 addresses and calls `addValidator` for each. The attacker now controls 100 validators, each with a score of 500, dominating the validator ecosystem for that agent without staking or voting.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Theft of validator rewards and manipulation of core upgrades
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Legitimate validators whose share is diluted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires admin intervention to blacklist or migrate

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing access control tests for validator registration

threats.title: Unbounded Loop in totalUptimeScore Causes DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: The `totalUptimeScore` function iterates over all registered validators. Because validator registration is permissionless, an attacker can register enough validators to cause this function to exceed the block gas limit.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `ValidatorRegistry.sol`, `totalUptimeScore` uses a `for` loop from `0` to `validatorCount(virtualId)`. Since `addValidator` in `AgentNftV2` has no access control (as noted in TECH-H004), an attacker can cheaply register thousands of dummy validators. Any contract or off-chain system that relies on calling `totalUptimeScore` will revert due to out-of-gas errors, causing a permanent Denial of Service for score-dependent features.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; unbounded loop pattern.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unbounded for-loop over an array that can be grown permissionlessly

preconditions: Attacker has registered enough validators to exceed block gas limit (~30M gas)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Iterates over `_validators[virtualId].length` which can be artificially inflated
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No limit on number of validators or pagination mechanism

analysis_questions: How much gas does one iteration of `validatorScore` consume?
How many validators are needed to hit the block gas limit?
validation_requirements: Calculate gas cost per loop iteration
Determine cost to execute attack on Base

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker writes a script to call `addValidator` 10,000 times for a target agent. The `_validators` array grows massive. When the protocol backend or a user calls `totalUptimeScore`, the transaction reverts, breaking reward distribution or ELO calculations.
prerequisites: Gas money to fund the registrations

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Disruption of protocol operations, potentially freezing rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All participants in the targeted agent ecosystem
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to implement pagination

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing gas limit tests for large validator sets

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Crucial to determine exact attack cost
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Can be executed gradually over time

threats.title: Sentinel Value Collision in AgentFactoryV4 Allows Duplicate Applications
threats.hypothesis_description: If `_nextId` is initialized to 0, the first application receives ID 0. The mapping `_tokenApplication[tokenAddr]` uses 0 as a sentinel value to check if a token is unregistered, allowing the first token to be registered multiple times.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentFactoryV4`, `initFromToken` checks `require(_tokenApplication[tokenAddr] == 0)`. It then assigns `_tokenApplication[tokenAddr] = id`. If `id` is 0 (which happens if `_nextId` starts at 0), the mapping stores 0. Subsequent calls with the same token will pass the require check, creating duplicate applications for the same token. This breaks the 1-to-1 mapping assumption and can lead to accounting errors during withdrawal or execution.
threats.config_evidence: Deployment scripts do not explicitly show `nextId_` for FactoryV4, but if it defaults to 0, the vulnerability is active.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Using 0 as a sentinel value for a mapping where 0 is also a valid ID

preconditions: The target token is the very first one registered (gets ID 0)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: `_tokenApplication[tokenAddr] == 0` cannot distinguish between 'unregistered' and 'registered with ID 0'
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should initialize `_nextId` to 1, or use a different sentinel (e.g., `type(uint256).max`)

analysis_questions: What is the actual initialized value of `_nextId` on mainnet?
Can the duplicate applications be exploited to drain funds?
validation_requirements: Check mainnet state of `_nextId`
Test duplicate registration flow

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker notices `_nextId` is 0. Attacker calls `initFromToken(tokenA)`. Application 0 is created. Attacker calls `initFromToken(tokenA)` again. Application 1 is created for the same token. This creates conflicting state in `_applicationToken` and `_tokenApplication`.
prerequisites: Factory must be freshly deployed or initialized with 0

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: State corruption, potential locked funds or double withdrawals depending on execution order

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for ID 0 boundary condition

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as multiple applications for the same token

threats.title: Tax Rate Underflow Enables Infinite Token Minting
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setProjectTaxRates` function lacks an upper bound check. If an admin sets the tax rate above 100%, the unchecked subtraction in `_taxProcessing` underflows, minting massive amounts of tokens to the recipient.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentToken.sol`, `setProjectTaxRates` allows the owner to set tax basis points to any `uint16` value (up to 65535, or 655%). If set above 10000, the calculated `tax` exceeds `sentAmount_`. In `_taxProcessing`, the code `amountLessTax_ -= tax` is inside an `unchecked` block. This causes `amountLessTax_` to underflow to near `type(uint256).max`. The recipient's balance is then increased by this massive amount, completely destroying the token's economics.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; logic flaw in bounds checking.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unchecked subtraction where subtrahend can be larger than minuend due to missing bounds check

preconditions: Admin or Factory calls `setProjectTaxRates` with a value > 10000
A transfer occurs that triggers the tax

analysis_questions: Is there any off-chain validation preventing the admin from setting high rates?
Does the underflow successfully update balances without reverting elsewhere?
validation_requirements: Set tax rate to 20000
Execute a transfer
Verify recipient receives ~uint256.max tokens

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised admin key or malicious insider calls `setProjectTaxRates(20000, 20000)`. They then execute a small sell transaction. The underflow mints them trillions of tokens, which they immediately dump into the Uniswap pool, draining all paired liquidity.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total drain of LP pool and destruction of token value
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders and liquidity providers

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing bounds testing for tax rate setters

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as massive token transfer event

threats.title: Founder Lock Bypass via Admin Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setMatureAt` function allows the admin to arbitrarily reduce the maturity timestamp, bypassing the time-lock intended to secure the founder's initial liquidity.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentVeToken.sol`, the founder's initial stake is locked until `matureAt` to provide economic security to the agent ecosystem. However, `setMatureAt` allows an `ADMIN_ROLE` holder to change this timestamp to any value, including a timestamp in the past. This breaks the trust assumption of the lock, allowing a colluding or compromised admin to let the founder withdraw and dump their LP tokens early.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; logic flaw in monotonic time enforcement.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Time-lock variable can be reduced arbitrarily by admin

preconditions: Admin colludes with founder or admin key is compromised

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should enforce that maturity can only be extended, not shortened

analysis_questions: Is the admin a multisig or a single EOA?
Can this be used as a rug-pull vector?
validation_requirements: Call `setMatureAt(0)` as admin
Verify founder can withdraw `initialLock` immediately

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The protocol advertises a 10-year lock on founder liquidity. A malicious admin calls `setMatureAt(0)`. The founder immediately calls `withdraw`, removes their LP tokens, and drains the Uniswap pool, rugging all investors.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of all initial liquidity backing the agent
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All investors in the specific agent

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for monotonic maturity extension

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as maturity reduction followed by withdrawal

threats.title: Permanent DAO Hijacking via setDAO
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setDAO` function allows the current DAO to change the DAO address to any arbitrary contract without validation. If an attacker gains temporary control of the DAO, they can permanently lock it to their own contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentNftV2.sol`, `setDAO` requires the caller to be the current DAO. If an attacker successfully executes the delegation overwrite attack (TECH-H001) to gain majority voting power, they can pass a proposal that calls `setDAO(virtualId, attackerContract)`. Because there is no validation that `newDAO` is a legitimate governor contract, the attacker's contract becomes the permanent DAO. They can then unilaterally control all DAO-gated functions (like `setCoreTypes` and `setTokenURI`) forever.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; missing validation on critical state update.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Critical address update without interface or legitimacy validation

preconditions: Attacker gains majority voting power in the current DAO (e.g., via TECH-H001)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: `info.dao = newDAO;` accepts any address, including EOAs or malicious contracts
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should verify `newDAO` implements `IGovernor` or restrict changes to a factory-approved list

analysis_questions: Can `setDAO` be chained with the delegation overwrite to achieve permanent takeover?
What is the impact of controlling `setCoreTypes` and `setTokenURI`?
validation_requirements: Simulate a DAO proposal calling `setDAO`
Verify the new DAO can execute restricted functions

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker steals voting power using TECH-H001. They create a proposal to call `setDAO` with their own EOA. Once passed, the attacker directly calls `setTokenURI` to deface the agent NFT and `setCoreTypes` to disrupt agent functionality, holding the agent hostage.
prerequisites: Ability to pass a DAO proposal

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of governance control and potential ransom of agent identity
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All stakeholders of the hijacked agent
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires protocol admin to intervene via `migrateVirtual`

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for DAO migration and validation

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Requires waiting for DAO voting period
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as a DAO proposal changing the DAO address
